speaker: interviewer
text: --'96. We are conducting an interview with Henry Weil, and this is take 2. How long were you at the factory, Mr. Weil?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Three months. Three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after you left there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, to my friend's. We were working together in the factory. I saw what happened in-- in the place. In-- in-- we said that we are not going back, you know, to the camp, because who knows what will be tomorrow?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to take-- we took some tools. I was the one, you know, who cut the electric wire. It was at night, winter, rain, and it was dark. And we-- and we went through the electric wires. We went in-- we were already in the forest. I was walking almost a whole night. No, I-- we didn't-- I didn't see nobody. But I heard some shots. Possible that first they were-- they were dead, you know. So you walked in different directions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't see him, you know, which directions in the forest. It was the first time, you know, in the forest over there. So I walked. I walked, and I walked, and I walked, you know, while it was still dark, you know, till already it was 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock, probably because it got a little lighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were wearing your striped uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wearing striped uniform. And from far away, I saw a bungalow, a small house. I went near, and I knocked in the window. So a man looked out like that, and he-- and he has in his hand a hatchet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I was afraid that he's going to kill me. He wants to kill me. I said, listen, I ran away from a concentration camp, from the factory and from the death camp. Please, let me live. My parents are already dead, my sisters, my whole family. You know, maybe I will have a chance, you know, to be alive. He didn't want it. He was-- he was against the Jews. So then his wife came out. I was lucky. His wife had mercy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And took away, first of all, she took away the hatchet from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, you will have to show-- in Polish, cause she was crying, they were crying, but crying in Polish, I could understand. You know, where do you want to go? I said, I would like to go where there's still a ghetto. So she said, which? The nearest city. And she said, the nearest city is Sandomierz. It's about 4 or 5 hours walking. So she told him, showed to him, oh, here is in the right direction to-- to Sandomierz. So he showed it to me. So she gave me something to drink also. That was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked, and I walked, and I walked about 4 or 5 hours, you know, and I came to Sandomierz. That was-- that was right. I came to Sandomierz, and I-- and I went straight to the ghetto. -- I-- [LAUGHS] I talked, you know, to the president, so he took me home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I ment-- and I mentioned that I was from Krakow, and that I was, you know, in a few other concentration camps. And then I ran away from Stalowa Wola. In the camp was a half an hour, 5 kilometers to walk, every day and every night, twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me money. And he gave me-- and I throw away the-- the uniform. And he said, listen, you know what I tell you? Tomorrow morning will be the same thing with us. That's what the president told me. They're going to send us to Auschwitz, or maybe some-- maybe to Chelmno, or maybe to Gross-Rosen. But we have to be prepared. So don't stay here. If you-- If you ran away already a couple of places, and you are alive, you know, don't stop. And where do you want to go? I said, I want to go to Krakow. In Krakow, I know. I know. So he gave me the money and he said, you know what? Go at night. Take the railroad, and don't talk to nobody. Sit down on the corner, if you have room, and make believe that you are sleeping. Just make believe that you are sleeping. All right, so I did. Why did you want  to go back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krakow? Because in Krakow, you know-- he mentioned to me, you know, Oskar Schindler mentioned to me, when you ever will be in Krakow, if you will be in Plaszow, if you will be in ghetto, I will be able to do something. Because I know that he has already the key from the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask you, at this point, how did you find out that your-- the rest of your family had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- my-- my-- somebody came, you know, to the place where we were standing. And he said, you know, he found-- found already the prosthesis from the leg. That's for sure your father's.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your mother and your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Chelmno, to a different concentration camp. They went all to straight to death, you know, to a grave, with my two-- my little two sisters, you know, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you left-- you left this--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left Sandomierz, and I went and stayed to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow, was a whole night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the trip to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to the railroad station, and I bought a ticket, and I went straight to Krakow, you know. It took a whole night, you know. I came to Krakow in the morning. I was walking in the street, and they said-- but everybody told me that I don't look like a Jew. But I was afraid to open my mouth, because the Polish people are anti-Semite. One thing, they know it. Because the Germans, they couldn't recognize are you a Jew, or you're a German, or you are English, or whatever. The-- the Polack, he pointed with the finger, Jew, Jew, Jew, Jew, Jew. And right away, they took, you know, to the cemetery over there, where we used to be there, in Plaszow. And from Plaszow, they built anti-Semite, anti-Jewish cemetery. They built, you know, barracks for us to Plaszow. The liquidation in Plaszow, you know, I mean the ghetto. Then we went, you know, to-- to-- to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you got to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do first, when you got to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went straight-- I didn't know what to do, so I went straight to-- to the ghetto. In the ghetto, I met already my cousin, Banchur, who was the factory. And Schindler came in a couple of times, Oskar Schindler, because he got already the key. And he said, you know, even the liquidation from Krakow or ghetto will be-- will-- the liquidation. And you will go, you know, to Jerozolimska, where the cemetery is, and you will stay over there. But from over there, they will make a list. They will make a list how many people will go to the factory working. And they will sleep over there by Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky that they're still in the ghetto. So I-- so my cousin Banchur asked Schindler, and he said-- because that was his factory-- make sure that Henry Weil is on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Banchur, in the factory, when we were working, you know, by Oskar Schindler, he helped me a lot. He helped me with money. And I could give the money to the Polacks, and they brought bread, and whatever I wanted, you know, Whatever I wanted. Butter, sometimes kielbasa. You know what that means. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And then-- and then we went from the ghetto to liquidation. From the ghetto, we went to Jerozolimska. That was a Jewish cemetery. They straighten out the stones and everything. The barracks was already in, and we were over there-- over there only maybe one year, till 1943, from '42 to '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor. Labor work. Everybody was doing labor work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the living conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad, because we had very bad SS Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they're very bad SS Men. But you know-- but Oskar Schindler was very smart. Oskar Schindler was like an actor. He was-- when he came, he was the best friend they could get. They were drinking together. They came-- he came to Oskar Schindler, to the office. And Oskar Schindler came here to-- to Jerozolimska, where we were there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when-- and so we were working and everything, you know. Then after-- it took a year, you know. Did you see these SS Men punish anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, that's-- I want to-- I want to-- to run away from the Jerozolimska, because they killed over there plenty of people. They came from the city. They were not-- not like Jewish people. They were hiding, and they killed on the spot, you know. We were-- we were working with the shovels, you know, to-- to dig graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Graves for whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the people they brought from outside, from the city, the Jewish people. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then so-- then over there, I try to escape, you know, from the Jerozolimska. I have to try to escape. But then Schindler thought, you know, that-- but I couldn't, because they were watching the other gate. Plenty SS men. So they gave me, naked, you know, 50 on my behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you tried to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: To escape. I was dead. For a nice couple of weeks, I had black marks. Black marks, you know. A nurse was every day, every day trying to help me out, you know, in the hospital. We had a small hospital over there, too. And nurses and something like that, you know. But all right, so I survive. After a year, they liquidate the concentration camp, Jerozolimska. And we all went. Was already-- we-- I was already on the list, you know, to go to-- Schindler's list.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 19-- in the ghetto. They made-- in the ghetto, they made the list. In ghetto. And he said, don't worry about it. Banchur helped me and Schindler helped me. To me, they were-- they were very nice, you know. Besides, he was my cousin, and money, he had plenty from the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sold it, you know. Because to the SS Men, Schindler said, listen, I don't care about the factory. But I want to help you, the SS. The SS Men ask, how can you help us? So he said-- he was very smart, Schindler-- I'll tell you how. We have the stores. The Polish people, they can't buy nothing. It's empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if the factory-- I will run the factory, you know. They will have plenty pots and pans, and everybody will buy it. And for the money, you can buy bullets and guns and everything what you need it. Because without guns, without anything, you can't go into the toilet. All right, so after there was the liquidation in Gross-- in the-- in Jerozolimska, we all--

speaker: interviewer
text: But what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. In 1943. So we went, all the men, even who was on the list also, to-- to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the camp. I mean, the liquidation, with the-- by trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food and water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Without food, without drink, they figure-- they figured, we'll be less, you know. We'll be less work for the SS-- for the SS. Because any-- regardless, they want to finish it up, all the Jews. Matter of fact, you know, 6 million, and 2 million children killed, you know. And he wants to go further. He wants to go to Russia too. But all right. But so the liquidation, you know, Jerozolimska, we went straight-- the men went to Gross-Rosen, and the women went, you know, to Auschwitz. But you know-- but Schindler gave-- gave them only one week, you can hold my people. I have a list. I have a copy, and you have a copy over there. To the-- to the big shots over there in Gross-Rosen. Just to go to the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it was like when you got to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross-Rosen was first miserable. Gross-Rosen was, you know-- in the beginning, first I-- it was labor. But then it became a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the barracks like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks, plenty-- plenty of people was pushing in, you know, and were sleeping on the floor. And plenty of them got sick, because from the cement-- cement floors. It was not from wood, nothing. No carpet. No carpeting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 500 went to Gross-Rosen, and after 10 days, you know, they send them to-- to Brinnlitz, to Schindler. Schindler-- Schindler's liquidation in Plaszow, you know, the factory. And he-- and he tried to get in Brinnlitz-- and that is in Czechoslovakia-- all the machineries and everything, you know what I mean? And to have them in order in Brinnlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the list also. But my brother was also in Gross-Rosen with me, together. He was not on the list. He was not on the list because he was already-- the list was already full.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and the women was one week in Auschwitz. And after the one week, you know, he let out the-- the 400 women to Brinnlitz, like Schindler wanted. Schindler had a big-- plenty to say. So when-- so I said, you know, I said to everybody, how about by brother? Even to Banchur, I told him, listen, where I will leave my brother? You know, I was in the army. I can do it much more than my brother, and he is older than me. Take-- make sure that he's on the list also.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So the last minute, I took off my-- my uniform, because the number, and he took off his uniform, and I-- and I gave it, and he put it on. And he put on, and I put on his. And he-- he went on my place. And he was in Brinnlitz like on my name, Henry Weil. And I was in Gross-Rosen. In Gross-Rosen, I went through plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross-Rosen, you know, we were working very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When were dead people, you know, I have to-- to-- graves to dig, and to make sure that everything is all right, and everything, you know. And it was impossible, impossible, impossible. I figured that I will not make it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one thing, I'm glad that we were only a couple of weeks in Gross-Rosen. After Gross-Rosen, the-- the liquidation from Gross-Rosen to Buchenwald, also in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make any selections  at Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross-Rosen, no one. The rest, everybody went to Buchenwald, because maybe they-- they had something in mind, whatever it is. Then Buchenwald. Buchenwald was also-- was first a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by train from Gross-Rosen to Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it was 7, 8 hours. Very slow, you know. And then from Buchenwald to Sonnenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? That's-- everything was in 19-- Buchenwald and Sonnenburg was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of the year? Was it summer or spring?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was already in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: In February, cold, you know, because, matter  of fact, the Americans-- I tell you. Then from Sonnenburg, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you went from Gross-Rosen to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Buchenwald. What was that-- when you got to Buchenwald--

speaker: interviewee
text: A labor camp. Labor camp. Hard work, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald, only labor. Labor, hard-- hard work. We're standing here outside in the cold weather. We were standing here for hours, you know, shivering.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the appels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: For the roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And after, they liquidate Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing like in Gross-Rosen. Only--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you slept on the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the floor. When they liquidate Buchenwald, they sent us to the last camp, Sonnenburg. In Sonnenburg--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Sonnenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also by train. You know, when we came to Sonnenburg, it was cold, and I didn't see, you know, that's over there near the house where they were-- the SS Men is looking, watching. I see that somebody went down, you know, into the basement by a small window, and he came out with a few potatoes. So when he came out, you know, the SS Man saw it, and he killed him. Two-- for two potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them, yeah. I was lucky, because I wanted to go next. Because the window was so small, we couldn't go together. I was lucky, sometime, you know, that I'm still-- I'm still here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm, at Sonnenburg. Like wood, you know. Something-- from wood, you know, very, very simp-- very primitive, you know. Barracks, you know. That was not, you know-- nobody could live a whole year, because no heat, nothing. What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Miserable. They gave us food, you know, that-- that you could-- you could take it out, give it back, you know. Sometimes, on Sunday, they gave us a piece of bread, you know. A piece-- a piece bread with-- with water. Very difficult. From Sonnenburg--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any acts of kindness between the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some of them, they said, you know, I will not make it. Maybe if I will be dead, maybe it will be much easier. Much easier, so I will forget the whole story, the whole thing, you know, what we have gone through after so many years. After so many years fighting, you know, for the life. And because, you know, that was no life. There's no life, nothing. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Listen, did any of the more religious people ever try to celebrate Shabbat or holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Matter of fact, that's-- when the-- the Germans came, sometimes you can see in the movie also, in the television, that you know-- that once in a while, they're showing how they-- how they burned the synagogues, and everything. You know what I mean. You couldn't have-- and here, if somebody wants to be a religious man, only-- only quiet, you know, that nobody will be able to see and to hear, if somebody is a religious man. And then the liquidation, Sonnenburg. The liquidation Sonnenburg. That was from a couple thousand, was only one road from east to west, and from both sides was forest that they called the Death March. In German, the Death March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing when you left to go on this Death March?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing, the same-- same uniform, same-- same stripes, you know, with the number and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what they gave us, you know, pair-- that pair of nothings, you know, just-- always cold, cold in the night and everything. But I-- when we walked, we walked and from both sides  were SS Men, maybe about 20 SS Men on one side, and 20 on the other side, and in the front. And we walked, you know-- and we walked, you know, from-- from east to west.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women on this Death March, or just men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just men. Just men. Just a camp for men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they didn't give us nothing. I don't know how good that I-- I need very little food at that time, you know, because I was always a bad eater. So we went in-- we went day and night, day and night, you know. And we couldn't go indoor. And probably we went west, because the Americans probably came from east.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see, as you were walking along?

speaker: interviewee
text: People. Dead people. Not because-- not-- not from-- from the gunshots. You know, they couldn't make it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I figured--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to take a tape break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, good.
